skill.name: iterate-pr
skill.description: Guides an AI coding agent through checking a pull request, reading CI and review feedback, fixing valid issues, committing changes, and waiting for checks to pass.
skill.summary: Automates the feedback, fix, push, and wait loop for pull requests.
skill.author: getsentry

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns. AI review confirms the real risk is the intentional GitHub CLI and git workflow, while Markdown backtick, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives. Risk remains medium because the skill instructs authenticated commands that can modify and push repository changes.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub and Git Command Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally instructs agents to run gh and git commands, including reading PR data, inspecting logs, committing, and pushing. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it can change repository state with authenticated credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown code blocks and align with the skill purpose. The risk is operational rather than malicious because no unauthorized endpoint or concealed execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for code execution syntax: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown formatting and command examples in SKILL.md. They are not executable skill code, although some examples instruct legitimate gh and git command use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown, and these locations are fenced command examples or inline code spans. No Ruby file, shell script, or hidden execution wrapper was present.

low_findings.title: Unquoted Branch Command Substitution
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with limited impact: The commands use shell substitution to insert the current branch name. This does not show malicious intent, but unusual branch names could cause argument splitting or confusing command behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit and branch-derived. It is a real shell robustness concern, but the surrounding workflow is normal PR automation.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts match ordinary Markdown words and workflow text. No cryptographic operation, host discovery, or data collection behavior appears in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only descriptive prose at these locations. There is no hash, cipher, network probe, or system enumeration code in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Modifying Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes git add, git commit, and git push instructions. These commands are expected, but marketplace users should understand that the workflow can publish code changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The repository-modifying commands are directly present in the skill. Their purpose is legitimate, but they create meaningful publication risk if used without review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated GitHub CLI Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires an authenticated gh installation and uses it to read PRs, checks, comments, and action logs. This grants access according to the user account permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The GitHub CLI dependency and commands are explicit. No abusive API use is shown, but authenticated repository access is a real capability.

content.user_title: Iterate Pull Requests Until CI Passes
content.value_statement: Pull request fixes can stall when CI failures and review comments arrive in several places. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a repeatable fix and verification loop.
actual_capabilities: Identifies the current pull request with GitHub CLI.
Checks CI status before reviewing comments.
Collects review, inline, issue, and bot feedback.
Reads failed GitHub Actions logs before making changes.
Guides targeted fixes, commits, pushes, and CI waiting.
Defines stop conditions for repeated failures or unclear feedback.
limitations: Requires authenticated GitHub CLI access in the repository.
Does not replace human judgment for unclear review requests.
Can push code changes if the agent is allowed to run commands.
Focused on GitHub pull requests and GitHub Actions workflows.

use_cases.title: Repair CI Failures
use_cases.description: A developer can ask the agent to inspect failed checks, read logs, fix valid issues, and wait for new results.

use_cases.title: Resolve Review Feedback
use_cases.description: A maintainer can gather human and bot comments, filter invalid feedback, and apply focused changes.

use_cases.title: Manage Release PR Readiness
use_cases.description: A release engineer can repeat checks until required statuses pass or a clear blocker needs escalation.

prompt_templates.title: Check This Pull Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple status review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iterate-pr skill to inspect the current pull request. Report CI status, review feedback, and any next action before changing files.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Failed Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to read failed check logs, identify real failures, make minimal fixes, and wait for CI after pushing.

prompt_templates.title: Address Reviews and Bots
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request covering comments and automated feedback.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr to gather human reviews, inline comments, and bot comments. Fix valid issues and explain any feedback you skip.

prompt_templates.title: Run Full PR Iteration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for the complete loop.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterate-pr until the pull request is ready. Commit and push focused fixes, stop after repeated failures, and ask for help on unclear decisions.

output_examples.input: Use this skill to check why my pull request is failing.
output_examples.output: The agent reports the PR number, failed checks, relevant log findings, and the smallest fix it plans to make.

output_examples.input: Address all review comments on this branch.
output_examples.output: The agent separates valid comments from outdated feedback, applies focused edits, and summarizes what changed.

output_examples.input: Keep iterating until CI is green.
output_examples.output: The agent repeats status checks, fixes new failures, pushes updates, and stops when all required checks pass.

best_practices: Review planned changes before allowing commits and pushes.
Use the skill from a clean branch with a linked pull request.
Ask the agent to explain skipped feedback and repeated failures.
anti_patterns: Do not run it with broad repository write access on untrusted branches.
Do not let it push changes without reviewing high-risk edits.
Do not use it when CI failures need product or architecture decisions.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explicitly requires authenticated gh access in the working repository.

faq.question: Can it push commits?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes git add, git commit, and git push commands.

faq.question: Does it work outside GitHub?
faq.answer: No. The documented commands use GitHub CLI and GitHub pull request APIs.

faq.question: Does it read CI logs?
faq.answer: Yes. It directs the agent to inspect failed GitHub Actions logs before fixing issues.

faq.question: How does it handle flaky or repeated failures?
faq.answer: It tells the agent to ask for help after the same failure persists for three attempts.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use normal repository controls. The skill relies on the permissions of the authenticated GitHub account.
